topic: Philosophy of Religion: Philosophical Issues & Questions
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Did Hume claim to have proved that God does not exist?
items.correct: No - Hume undermined the force of the design and causal arguments, but his Dialogues end ambiguously; weakening proofs is not the same as proving atheism.
items.misconception: Hume's criticisms amount to a demonstration that there is no God.
items.random_wrong: Hume argued the design argument was completely irrefutable.
items.why: Students overstate sceptical conclusions; precise evaluation distinguishes 'argument fails' from 'conclusion false'.

items.q: Does the cosmological argument say 'everything has a cause, so God must have one too'?
items.correct: No - classical versions argue that things in motion or contingent things require explanation, concluding in a necessary, uncaused being; that being is not another contingent item needing a cause.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument is refuted simply by asking 'then what caused God?'
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument is based on the beauty of sunsets.
items.why: The strawman version invites a one-line refutation; engaging the real argument about contingency is what gains analysis marks.

items.q: Does evolution automatically destroy every design argument?
items.correct: It targets Paley-style biological design, but fine-tuning versions appeal to the constants of physics, so evaluation must match the criticism to the version of the argument.
items.misconception: Darwin ended all possible design reasoning about the universe.
items.random_wrong: Evolution disproved the cosmological argument about motion.
items.why: Students fire Darwin at every target; matching objections to specific argument forms is a key discriminator at A-level.

items.q: What does Paley's watch analogy argue?
items.correct: Complexity and purpose in nature imply a designer, just as a watch implies a watchmaker
items.misconception: Time proves the universe is eternal
items.why: Correct answer: Complexity and purpose in nature imply a designer, just as a watch implies a watchmaker. “Time proves the universe is eternal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which argument for God's existence is a priori?
items.why: Correct answer: The ontological argument. “The design argument” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: That than which nothing greater can be conceived
items.misconception: The first cause of motion only
items.why: It's That than which nothing greater can be conceived. “The first cause of motion only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
